meeting_purpose_blurb: Council learns about topics in a more informal meeting. The public may observe via Zoom; no public comment is taken.
lead_headline: Arvada PD Unveils 'ARCTIC' Real-Time Info Hub Ahead of April Contract Vote

type_specific.meeting_summary: The Arvada City Council received an informational briefing on the Arvada Real Time Information Center (ARCTIC), a new citywide hub that will consolidate feeds from drones, surveillance cameras, license plate readers, and body-worn cameras to improve emergency response and operational efficiency across multiple city departments. Chief Brady and his team explained the program's technology, staffing, legal framework, and phased implementation plan, noting that a formal Axon contract covering three drone-first-responder units and related equipment will be brought to council for a vote on April 21. No formal vote was taken at this session.
type_specific.lead_headline: Arvada PD Unveils 'ARCTIC' Real-Time Info Hub Ahead of April Contract Vote

briefings.topic: Arvada Real Time Information Center (ARCTIC) — Program Overview, Technology & Funding
prose_paragraphs: Chief Brady introduced the Arvada Real Time Information Center, branded 'ARCTIC,' as a centralized operations hub that consolidates live feeds from drone-first-responders (DFRs), more than 400 existing city surveillance cameras, automated license plate readers (ALPRs), body-worn cameras, and eventually traffic cameras. Located in the former dispatch center on the second floor of police headquarters, the ARCTIC currently houses two workstations staffed by two newly regraded Arctic technicians. The system is built around a contract with Axon, which sub-contracts drone hardware to a company called Skydio (the Skydio X10 model), with software integration handled through a platform called Fusus. The formal contract will come before council for a vote on April 21.

The ARCTIC is designed to serve the entire city, not just the police department. Chief Brady outlined use cases spanning emergency management (wildfire smoke detection via AI-enabled tower cameras, flood damage assessment), structure fires (sending live drone feeds to an incident commander before units arrive, as demonstrated by a Lakewood example showing 2.5 minutes of advance intelligence), infrastructure monitoring (traffic patterns, water-line breaks, power outages, I-70 structural damage assessment), parks management (crowd monitoring, sprinkler inspections, homeless-camp status checks), and police operations (officer safety, fugitive tracking, clearing calls for service remotely). Lakewood PD, cited as a peer agency, has cleared 41% of calls for service without deploying an officer since activating its DFR program. Greeley, using only intersection cameras without a DFR, cleared approximately 300 calls with that technology alone. The department will deploy three DFRs: two at City Hall headquarters and one at the Lake Arbor (Adam) station, providing partial but not full city coverage at launch. Each drone has a roughly 22-minute battery life, necessitating the two-unit rotation at headquarters. Funding is drawn from three sources: regrading two existing positions (a civilian accident investigator slot and a neighborhood-services administrative role) at net savings, approximately $100,000 in seizure funds for the physical buildout of the ARCTIC space, and $500,000 in reimbursements from Jefferson County School District for school resource officers, which the department is redirecting to cover program costs.

Council members raised several open questions about the program's maturity and future trajectory. Staffing was a recurring concern: with only two technicians splitting an early-swing schedule, council members worried about coverage gaps and the risk of an operator being focused on a DFR flight while missing another incident elsewhere in the city. Chief Brady acknowledged the ideal staffing would be at least four technicians plus a supervisor to cover roughly 20 hours per day, and noted that officers on light duty could supplement the team. A question about data fusion prompted discussion of the Fusus platform's ability to aggregate multiple data sources, with the NYPD fusion center cited as a long-term model. Councilmember Ambrose raised a pointed concern that all public community engagement meetings are scheduled on or after April 21, the same date as the contract vote, giving the public no opportunity to influence the procurement. The Chief acknowledged this and indicated dates could be revisited. Phase one of implementation will cover DFR deployment, integration with the computer-aided dispatch (CAD) system, and body-worn cameras; phase two will incorporate existing city cameras and new strategic camera installations (including towers on Highway 72 and Highway 68); and phase three will integrate ALPR technology citywide.

presenter_quotes.speaker: Chief Brady
presenter_quotes.text: essentially the Arctic is a hub of information that allows us to provide a real and immediate information to those in the field who need it most.
presenter_quotes.context_excerpt: And so, I mean, essentially the Arctic is a hub of information that allows us to provide a real and immediate information to those in the field who need it most. That's essentially what it is and I'll go into the specifics on it. But you may ask, why do we need the Arctic?

presenter_quotes.speaker: Chief Brady
presenter_quotes.text: I think they're selling themselves way too short. This has more benefit than just the PD. It has benefit for all city services.
presenter_quotes.context_excerpt: I think you will you will have heard this term, a real time crime center, in other cities. I think they're selling themselves way too short. This has more benefit than just the PD. It has benefit for all city services and I'm going to dive into those.

presenter_quotes.speaker: Chief Brady
presenter_quotes.text: at the end of the day the PD has been able to absorb this within our own budget.
presenter_quotes.context_excerpt: So we're basically getting 100 we're getting 500,000 that we're applying from the reimbursement from the school district to the Arctic program. And so at the end of the day the PD has been able to absorb this within our own budget.

presenter_quotes.speaker: Unidentified
presenter_quotes.text: aerial surveillance uh such as this is perfectly legal, not only in the state of Colorado, but in the United States.
presenter_quotes.context_excerpt: Uh I'll just come out and say it is uh aerial surveillance uh such as this is perfectly legal, not only in the state of Colorado, but in the United States. In fact, we are video aerial surveillance, I keep saying video surveillance, aerial surveillance uh was first litigated back in uh the 1980s in the form of helicopters.

presenter_quotes.speaker: Unidentified
presenter_quotes.text: the drones uh fly at a altitude that is actually owned and owned and operated, for lack of better description, by the federal government, the FAA.
presenter_quotes.context_excerpt: In fact, uh the drones uh fly at a altitude that is actually owned and owned and operated, for lack of better description, by the federal government, the FAA. So, our drones have to be registered with the FAA. I know uh Jason has been working on that application.

presenter_quotes.speaker: Unidentified
presenter_quotes.text: The Fourth Amendment has a number of recognized or known exceptions. One such such exception that immediately comes into play there is what is known as hot pursuit.
presenter_quotes.context_excerpt: The Fourth Amendment has a number of recognized or known exceptions. One such such exception that immediately comes into play there is what is known as hot pursuit. As officers do not violate the Fourth Amendment when they are in literal hot pursuit of a suspect.

presenter_quotes.speaker: Chief Brady
presenter_quotes.text: one of the things that the sector commanders I have talked about is having more of a regular cadence of meetings with the communities like this um regardless of whether or not it's a hot topic and and regardless of whether one person attends or 20.
presenter_quotes.context_excerpt: So, one of the things that the sector commanders I have talked about is having more of a regular cadence of meetings with the communities like this um regardless of whether or not it's a hot topic and and regardless of whether one person attends or 20. Being able just to have that opportunity.

presenter_quotes.speaker: Unidentified
presenter_quotes.text: the software has a transparency portal. So, every flight that this uh drone takes will be publicly available to where they get on there on a public-facing and see where the drone went will be listed here.
presenter_quotes.context_excerpt: The other good thing about this is is the uh software has a transparency portal. So, every flight that this uh drone takes will be publicly available to where they get on there on a public-facing and see where the drone went will be listed here. What time? Yeah, it'll be a part of the the whole website.

presenter_quotes.speaker: Unidentified
presenter_quotes.text: the data is ours. So it's not shared.
presenter_quotes.context_excerpt: So I assume for this system it would be those same list of agencies that would have access and we would be transparent about that. And you know, you put that the data only stays in the system for 14 days and then after that you have like the logs of where it was flown, but you don't actually have the data, right? So the good thing with this product, the data is ours. So it's not shared.

external_resources.title: Axon Contract (drone-first-responder program, body-worn cameras, tasers, fleet cameras, evidence.com, Draft One, virtual reality headsets)
Skydio X10 Drone-First-Responder Hardware Specifications (Skydio Dock for X10)
Fusus Software Platform — Axon/Skydio Integration and Data Aggregation
Lakewood Police Department Drone-First-Responder Program — Calls-for-Service Data (41% cleared without officer dispatch)
Greeley Police Department Camera Program — Calls-for-Service Clearance Data (~300 calls cleared via intersection cameras)
Jefferson County School District SRO Reimbursement Agreement (50% cost reimbursement for school resource officers)
Axon Drone Transparency Portal (public-facing flight log system)
Arvada Report (February–March issue) — Full-page article on the ARCTIC program
Arvada Police Department Annual Report — Technology and Innovation section (ARCTIC, ALPRs, Draft One, facial recognition)
FAA Drone Registration and Flight Plan Records (public record requirement for DFR operations)
NYPD Fusion Center — Referenced as a long-term model for data integration

questions_raised: How many drones will the city operate, and what geographic coverage will three DFRs provide?
How does the hot-pursuit Fourth Amendment exception apply when a drone follows a fleeing suspect through private backyards?
Can council and the public track drone flight paths, and how does the obstacle-avoidance and horizon-level camera design limit incidental recording?
What is the life expectancy of the drones and docking stations, and is a hardware refresh built into the contract?
How will the department keep pace with software updates given the IT department's existing workload?
Will the city eventually expand drone coverage to the entire city, including remote areas like Canyon Pines?
Are the drones marked with red and blue lights or decals so the public can identify them as police assets?
With only two technicians on a split early-swing schedule, how will the department avoid coverage gaps, and is 24-hour staffing the goal?
If a technician is actively piloting a DFR, who is monitoring the rest of the camera feeds for other incidents?
Is the department exploring data-fusion technology to algorithmically surface incidents rather than relying solely on human operators?
Why are all community engagement meetings scheduled on or after the April 21 contract vote, giving the public no chance to shape the procurement?
What does the audit process look like for ensuring DFR flights are tied to legitimate law enforcement calls rather than general surveillance?
Which third-party agencies, if any, can access ARCTIC video data, and how long is footage retained before deletion?
Would the availability of DFRs prompt the department to revisit or expand its partial vehicle-pursuit policy?
How will the department communicate to the public what constitutes 'general surveillance' and what safeguards exist against it?
Will the department use DFRs for lower-level nuisance complaints such as fireworks violations or short-term rental parties, and how will it prioritize call types?
Could the city use its drone assets at public events or school career fairs to build community familiarity with the technology?
Is the department sharing more body-cam and drone footage on social media to proactively demonstrate transparency, as some peer agencies do?

No formal vote was taken at this session.
